title: Fix sandbox bypass in launch path
description: In launcher.go, the sandbox is only applied when rc.Context.Sandbox != nil && !rc.Context.Sandbox.Disabled. If the config has no sandbox: block, Sandbox is nil and the sandbox is skipped entirely. Yet PolicyFromConfig(nil, ...) correctly returns DefaultPolicy — it is just never called.
design:
1. In launcher.go Launch(), remove the nil guard on line 162
2. Replace with unconditional call to sandbox.PolicyFromConfig(rc.Context.Sandbox, ...)
3. Keep the if policy != nil check (nil only when sandbox: false)
4. Write tests first (TDD)
acceptance_criteria:
- go test ./internal/launcher/ -run TestLaunch_NoSandboxBlock_AppliesDefaults -v passes
- go test ./internal/launcher/ -run TestLaunch_SandboxFalse_NoSandbox -v passes
- Done when: launch path applies DefaultPolicy when config has no sandbox: block
notes: Files: internal/launcher/launcher.go, internal/launcher/launcher_test.go. Spec ref: Change 1, DD-18

title: Fix sandbox bypass in passthrough path
description: The execAgent() function in passthrough.go execs the agent without applying any OS sandbox. When aide --yolo is used without a config file, the agent runs with full filesystem access despite the warning claiming the sandbox is active. This allows reading SSH private keys, AWS credentials, and other sensitive files.
design:
1. Change `execAgent` signature to accept `cwd string` parameter
2. Add imports: `sandbox`, `aidectx`, `os/exec`
3. In `execAgent`, before exec:
   - Get homeDir, tempDir, projectRoot via `aidectx.ProjectRoot(cwd)`
   - Create RuntimeDir via `NewRuntimeDir()`
   - Call `CleanStale()`
   - Build policy via `sandbox.DefaultPolicy(...)`
   - Create exec.Cmd and apply sandbox via `sandbox.NewSandbox().Apply()`
   - Exec via `l.Execer.Exec(cmd.Path, cmd.Args, cmd.Env)`
4. Update all callers of `execAgent` to pass `cwd`:
   - `Passthrough()` line 68
   - `Passthrough()` line 89
5. Write tests first (TDD)
acceptance_criteria:
- `go test ./internal/launcher/ -run TestPassthrough_AppliesSandbox -v` passes
- `go test ./internal/launcher/ -run TestPassthrough_ExecAgent_UsesCwd -v` passes
- `go test ./internal/launcher/ -run TestPassthrough_NoOptOut_AlwaysSandboxed -v` passes
- Integration: `go test ./internal/sandbox/ -run TestPassthrough_SandboxDeniesSSHKeys -v -tags integration` passes
- Done when: passthrough path wraps agent execution in sandbox-exec on macOS
notes: Files: internal/launcher/passthrough.go, internal/launcher/passthrough_test.go. Spec ref: Change 1, DD-18. NOTE: cleanup is best-effort since syscall.Exec replaces the process. Runtime dir persists until CleanStale() on next invocation.

title: Sandbox bypass: git-remote silently grants SSH-agent escape without ssh capability
description: When the 'ssh' capability is OFF but 'git-remote' is on (typically auto-detected via .git/config marker), the git-remote guard still emits: (1) SSH_AUTH_SOCK env passthrough, (2) network-outbound to the ssh-agent unix socket, (3) network-outbound to TCP *:22. This lets a sandboxed agent run 'git push' over SSH and have ssh-agent (running outside the sandbox) sign the challenge — fully bypassing the user's mental model that 'SSH access requires the ssh capability'. Same architectural class as the MCP/desktop-commander IPC bypass.
design:
DESIGN — ssh capability split & port-flex.

ARCHITECTURE
1. Move SSH-agent escape primitives out of git-remote guard, into a new ssh guard wired via 'ssh' capability:
   - SSH_AUTH_SOCK env passthrough
   - network-outbound to ssh-agent unix socket (path from $SSH_AUTH_SOCK, skip if unset)
   - ~/.ssh read access
   - network-outbound TCP on resolved SSH ports (see PORT RESOLUTION)
2. git-remote guard keeps only HTTPS-transport primitives:
   - TCP *:443
   - ~/.config/git-credential-manager (read)
   - deny ~/.git-credentials (defense in depth)
   - drop SSH_AUTH_SOCK from EnvAllow
3. git-remote stays auto-detected via .git/config marker. ssh remains explicit-only (no marker).

PORT RESOLUTION (Option 1 — deny-default)
The ssh guard resolves an allowed port set as the union of:
  A. Parsed from ~/.ssh/config — Host/Port directives for any host referenced by a current git remote (or all Host blocks if cheaper).
  B. Parsed from .git/config — ssh://host:PORT/... style remotes.
  C. Env override — AIDE_SSH_PORTS=22,2222 (comma-separated, validated as 1-65535 ints).
  D. .aide.yaml capability override — capabilities.ssh.ports: [22, 2222].
Default when none declared: [22].
Final set deduped; emits one (allow network-outbound (remote tcp "*:N")) per port.
Ports not in the resolved set stay denied — no wildcard fallback.

ACCEPTANCE
- Test: git-remote enabled, ssh disabled → profile has NO SSH_AUTH_SOCK env, NO unix-socket allow, NO *:22 allow, NO ~/.ssh read; HAS *:443 allow.
- Test: ssh enabled, git-remote disabled → profile has SSH_AUTH_SOCK env, unix-socket allow, *:22 allow, ~/.ssh read; NO *:443 allow.
- Test: both enabled → all primitives present, no duplicates.
- Test: ssh enabled + ~/.ssh/config has 'Port 2222' for a host present in .git/config remotes → profile allows *:22 AND *:2222.
- Test: ssh enabled + .git/config has ssh://host:2222/repo → profile allows *:22 AND *:2222.
- Test: ssh enabled + AIDE_SSH_PORTS=22,2222 env at profile-build time → profile allows both.
- Test: ssh enabled + .aide.yaml capabilities.ssh.ports: [22, 2222] → profile allows both.
- Test: union of all sources dedupes correctly.
- Test: invalid AIDE_SSH_PORTS values (non-numeric, out-of-range) → ignored with skip log, not crash.
- Existing git-remote guard tests updated for new boundary.
acceptance_criteria: Test: with 'git-remote' enabled and 'ssh' disabled, generated profile MUST NOT contain SSH_AUTH_SOCK env passthrough, MUST NOT contain unix-socket allow, MUST NOT contain TCP *:22 allow. Test: with both enabled, all SSH primitives present. Test: with only 'ssh' enabled (no git-remote), agent-socket + port 22 allowed but no port 443. Existing git-remote tests updated.
notes: Folded AIDE-osf (discoverability) into this issue: ship together as one PR to avoid a UX cliff where the security fix makes 'git push' fail with no hint. Adds: (1) updated capability descriptions for ssh / git-remote / github naming the operations they unlock; (2) detection-time hint when git-remote auto-detects ssh:// remotes and ssh capability is off — surfaced via aide status, launch banner, and aide setup. Hints are advisory only, never auto-grant.

title: Mode A: sandbox-enforce mode (seatbelt/landlock rules locking outbound to proxy port)
description:
Implement `network_filter.enforce: sandbox` — emit seatbelt rules (and Landlock when Linux parity lands) that deny all outbound TCP except to the proxy's loopback port + DNS. Closes T10 (adversarial-agent network egress) at the kernel level. Default `env` mode is unaffected.

**Parent epic:** AIDE-dpe
**Design context:** docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-02-network-filter-egress-sandbox-design.md (sandbox-enforce mode section, to be added)

## Scope
- New `enforce` config field: `env` (default) | `sandbox`
- Seatbelt rule emission: deny network-outbound except 127.0.0.1:<proxy-port> + DNS (UDP/TCP 53, 853 for DoT)
- Capability composition: document interaction with capabilities that need non-proxy egress (k8s cluster IPs, custom infra) — user must explicitly extend the allowlist
- Threat-model docs update: T10/network-egress moves from out-of-scope to closed-when-enforce-sandbox
- Linux Landlock equivalent deferred until Linux sandbox parity epic catches up

## Why separate from AIDE-dpe.4
Capability integration ships the `env` path day 1. Sandbox-enforce is the strict opt-in upgrade — same allowlist, additional kernel enforcement. Lands after the env path is proven.

title: Mode A: MCP-server env inheritance verification harness
description:
The whole point of Mode A is that MCP servers spawned by the agent inherit HTTPS_PROXY etc. Build a verification harness that spawns each supported agent + MCP server, makes outbound requests, and confirms the proxy saw them. Failure here means the feature does not actually work.

**Parent epic:** AIDE-dpe
**Design context:** docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-02-network-filter-egress-sandbox-design.md (Coverage and verification section)

title: Mode A: capability integration (--with network-filter, .aide.yaml schema)
description:
Wire the network filter into aide's capability system. CLI flag, .aide.yaml schema additions for `network_filter` block (mode/backend/allow/deny/default_action), trust-gate interaction, composition with other capabilities.

**Parent epic:** AIDE-dpe
**Design context:** docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-02-network-filter-egress-sandbox-design.md (Configuration surface section)

title: Mode A: lifecycle supervision (start, health, fail-closed restart, cleanup)
description:
Aide must supervise the proxy: start before agent, health-check (CONNECT to a sentinel), fail-closed on death, restart with capped backoff, surface failures, kill on agent exit. This supervision pattern is reused by Mode B and K8s filter.

**Parent epic:** AIDE-dpe
**Design context:** docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-02-network-filter-egress-sandbox-design.md (Lifecycle and supervision section)

title: Mode A: implement in-tree Go connect-filtering proxy (Filter)
description:
Implement the Filter per the design (depends on AIDE-dpe.1 design task). Pure Go, no MITM, no CA. Listens on loopback random port, handles CONNECT and plain HTTP.

**Key decisions from brainstorm (see design doc Brainstorm decisions log):**
- Squid `dstdomain`-compatible matcher: `map[string]bool` × 2, walk FQDN domain hierarchy. Zero deps. Use `golang.org/x/net/publicsuffix` for input normalization only.
- `default: allow` is the actual default. `default: deny` triggers curated agent-aware allow-list.
- Deny rules always win over allow.
- IP literals follow `default` policy; `allow_ips` field for explicit IP/CIDR.
- Trust the CONNECT target. No SNI peek. No TLS bytes read.
- Listener on loopback random port; address written to agent env at session start.
- One structured decision-log line per request to ~/.local/state/aide/network-filter/<session>.log.

**Parent epic:** AIDE-dpe
**Design context:** docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-02-network-filter-egress-sandbox-design.md (Mode A section)

title: Mode A: design connect-filter internals (rule engine, default policy, decision log) — DONE
description:
Brainstorm + design the in-tree Go SNI proxy: listener lifecycle, CONNECT vs plain-HTTP handling, hostname matching (exact + glob), default-action policy, denial response shape (403 body, log line), port assignment.

**Parent epic:** AIDE-dpe
**Design context:** docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-02-network-filter-egress-sandbox-design.md (Mode A section)

Run `/superpowers:brainstorming` against this task before implementation.
notes:
Brainstorm complete 2026-05-03. Outcomes baked into design doc (docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-02-network-filter-egress-sandbox-design.md, Brainstorm decisions log section).

Decisions:
- Q1: default: allow; curated agent-aware defaults only when default: deny
- Q2: Squid dstdomain syntax; zero-deps map-based implementation
- Q3: Trust CONNECT target; no SNI peek; TLS validation is OS responsibility
- Q4: IP literals follow default policy; revisit later
- Q5: Loopback random port
- Q6: Structured per-decision log file; no live TUI
- Architecture: Filter + Inspector split; Mode B chains through Mode A's Filter
- Enforcement: new env|sandbox modes; sandbox locks outbound at kernel level
- Backend rename: sni → connect

Design doc rewritten with full threat model, architecture diagrams, configuration surface, and brainstorm decisions log. Ready for AIDE-dpe.2 (implementation).

title: Network Filter — Mode A: connect-filtering proxy (Filter)
description:
Opt-in hostname-level egress allowlist for the agent process and its MCP servers. No CA install, no body decryption, zero runtime deps. Implements the `Filter` half of the Filter+Inspector split.

**Design:** docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-02-network-filter-egress-sandbox-design.md (Mode A section)

## Scope
- In-tree Go connect-filtering proxy (handles HTTP CONNECT + plain HTTP)
- Squid `dstdomain`-compatible hostname rules (zero-deps map-based matcher)
- `default: allow|deny` policy with deny-rules-always-win
- Agent-aware curated defaults loaded only when `default: deny`
- IP-literal handling per default policy + `allow_ips` opt-in
- Structured per-decision log to ~/.local/state/aide/network-filter/<session>.log
- `enforce: env|sandbox` modes (env default; sandbox locks outbound at OS level)
- Per-process env wiring (HTTPS_PROXY, HTTP_PROXY, NO_PROXY)
- Lifecycle supervision (start, health, fail-closed restart, cleanup)
- Capability integration (`--with network-filter`)
- Coverage tests verifying MCP servers inherit proxy env

## Why first
Closes the MCP-escape gap (memory: project_mcp_sandbox_escape.md) with no CA trust complications. Mode B and the K8s filter chain through this Filter, so it must exist first.

## Out of scope
Body inspection, prompt-injection scanning, K8s/SQL filtering — separate epics.

## Brainstorm decisions
See design doc Brainstorm decisions log. Backend rename: `sni` → `connect` (we never read SNI).

title: Unify DetectProject with Marker-based DetectEvidence
description: internal/capability/detect.go:DetectProject is a hardcoded if-ladder over 15 capabilities using ad-hoc helpers (fileExists/dirExists/containsInFile/hasFileWithExtension). internal/capability/detect_variants.go:DetectEvidence does the same job uniformly via Marker rules on Variant. Node, Ruby, and Java follow-up plans will multiply the duplication — each will reimplement file-marker detection from scratch. Re-express DetectProject as Markers on top-level Capability so DetectEvidence becomes the single scanner before those plans land.
design:
TDD order:
1. Snapshot current DetectProject output against fixture project trees via golden-style test in detect_test.go (capture exact suggestion order per fixture).
2. Add DirExists marker kind (or elevate dir-check into a new Marker field).
3. Declare the 15 built-in capabilities' top-level detection rules as []Marker on Capability in internal/capability/builtin.go.
4. Reimplement DetectProject as: for each Capability in registry, run markers; on any match emit the name. Preserve determinism.
5. Run golden test unchanged.
6. Retire hasFileWithExtension/hasFileWithExtensionOneLevelDeep/hasYAMLWithAPIVersion/checkYAMLsForAPIVersion if their semantics are now expressed via markers; otherwise keep as marker primitives.
acceptance_criteria: All capability package tests pass; DetectProject output identical on every fixture; builtin.go holds detection rules declaratively. Deleting one of the ad-hoc helpers must not regress any test.
notes: Pattern: Replace Conditional with Polymorphism / Consolidate Duplicate Conditional Fragments. Blocks node/ruby/java variant follow-up plans. Couple with the Marker-as-interface issue — DirExists would be the 4th parallel switch arm that motivates polymorphism.
close_reason: AIDE-c7o: merged to main via 7 commits (ee8b862 → e579dbc) — DetectProject rewritten as registry loop, 8 ad-hoc helpers deleted, 2 new marker kinds (DirExists/GlobContains), fs.FS throughout, golden test unchanged. AIDE-2kg: resolved as side-effect — variant.go's containsInBoundedFileFS is now the single bounded-read primitive with one 64KB cap.

title: Re-enable sandbox as default-on in launcher

title: Redesign aide setup with context reuse
description: Rewrite aide setup to detect existing contexts and present options: [1-N] Add this folder to an existing context (just appends match rule), [N+1] Create new context (current wizard), [N+2] Inherit from existing + customize (copy agent/secrets/env, let user override). If no contexts exist, go straight to the create-new flow.
design: 1. Load config, get existing contexts 2. If contexts exist: show numbered list with summary (agent, match count, env count), plus 'Create new' and 'Inherit + customize' options 3. If user picks existing context: call askMatchRule, append rule, write config, done 4. If 'Create new': run current 4-step wizard 5. If 'Inherit + customize': pick source context, copy its fields, prompt for overrides (agent, secrets, env), create new context with CWD match 6. If no contexts exist: straight to create-new flow

For git: ask 'Git remote URL pattern (e.g. github.com/company/*):'

title: aide setup — guided end-to-end wizard
description: New command: aide setup. Interactive wizard that does everything in one flow for the current directory: 1. Choose or create agent 2. Create or select secrets file 3. Wire secrets keys to env vars 4. Bind folder. Replaces the need to run use + secrets create + env set in the right order.
design:
1. Detect CWD, check if already has a matching context
2. If yes, offer to reconfigure or add env vars
3. If no: ask for agent (show detected + configured), create context with CWD match
4. Ask: Set up secrets? If yes: list existing secrets files or create new one
5. After secrets file selected/created, show keys and ask which to wire as env vars
6. For each selected key, ask for env var name (suggest UPPER_CASE version of key)
7. Write everything to config in one WriteConfig call
8. Print summary and aide which output

1. aide setup in a new directory creates full working config
2. aide setup in already-configured directory offers reconfigure
3. All env vars written use correct --from-secret templates
4. aide which after setup shows correct agent and context

2. If error contains 'map has no entry for key', extract the key name and context, print: Context X env var Y references secret key Z which is not in secrets file. Run: aide secrets keys <name> to see available keys

2. aide launch with no secrets_file configured shows actionable message with aide env set --from-secret suggestion

When --from-secret KEY is explicit, still validate the key exists and auto-set secrets_file if missing.

2. Check context.SecretsFile; if empty, scan SecretsDir for *.enc.yaml, present numbered list, let user pick via stdin
3. Set context.SecretsFile to selected file
4. Decrypt file, get keys, present numbered list
5. User picks key, generate template
6. If --from-secret has a value, still check secrets_file and auto-set if needed; validate key exists in file
7. Write config
acceptance_criteria:
1. aide env set API_KEY --from-secret (no value) prompts for file then key selection
2. After selection, context.SecretsFile is set and env var template is correct
3. aide env set API_KEY --from-secret mykey auto-sets secrets_file if missing and validates key exists

title: Capability discoverability: self-documenting descriptions + detection-time hints
description: After AIDE-4j4 splits ssh out of git-remote, users with only git-remote enabled and SSH-style remotes will see git push fail with a generic network error and no hint that they need to enable the ssh capability. Capability descriptions today are terse ('SSH keys and agent') and don't mention common compositions ('required for git over SSH'). There's no surfacing of capability dependencies at detection or launch time.
design: (1) Update built-in capability Descriptions to name the operations they unlock and the common compositions: 'ssh' explicitly mentions git-over-SSH/scp/rsync; 'git-remote' explicitly names HTTPS-only and points to ssh for SSH; 'github' notes the env-token path. (2) Add a hint mechanism: when git-remote auto-detects via .git/config marker, parse remote URLs once; if any are ssh:// or git@ style and ssh capability is not active, emit a structured hint surfaced in aide status, launch banner, aide setup, and aide which. (3) Optionally add a Notes/CommonCombinations field on Capability for aide cap show to render. Hints are advisory only — never auto-grant.
acceptance_criteria: Test: aide cap show ssh output mentions 'git over SSH'. Test: in repo with ssh:// remote and only git-remote enabled, aide status output contains a hint string referencing 'ssh' capability. Test: same condition but with ssh capability also enabled → no hint. Test: HTTPS-only remotes → no SSH hint. Test: hints never block launch and never auto-enable capabilities.

title: aide status: full pre-launch capability/sandbox preview with provenance
description: There is no single command that answers 'if I launch aide right now in this directory with these flags, what does the agent get?' aide status shows summary, sandbox show is terse, sandbox test dumps unreadable raw .sb. Users have to launch and read the banner to know what's actually granted — and the banner truncates. This contributed to the SSH-agent bypass bug (AIDE-4j4): a 'SSH agent socket: REACHABLE — granted by git-remote (auto-detected)' line in status would have made the surprise visible pre-launch.
design: Extend 'aide status' (or add 'aide status --full' / 'aide preview') to print the resolved view: (1) capabilities with provenance — context-config | marker-auto-detected | --with flag | --without overrides; (2) aggregated readable paths; (3) aggregated writable paths; (4) aggregated env passthrough list; (5) network rules — TCP ports, unix sockets (incl. ssh-agent socket if SSH_AUTH_SOCK is forwarded), network mode; (6) active guards with one-line summary of what each emits (count of allow/deny rules + key resources). All of this should be derivable from existing capability resolution + guard Rules() output — no new resolution logic, just reporting.
acceptance_criteria: Running the command in a repo with an [remote] in .git/config shows: (a) the auto-detected capability list including any ssh-related grants, (b) explicit 'SSH agent socket reachable: yes/no' line tied to the granting capability, (c) full readable + writable path lists with source attribution. Output stable enough to grep for assertions in tests.

Implement the hetty Inspector.

**Critical first step:** verify hetty supports upstream-proxy chaining (the equivalent of mitmproxy's `--mode upstream:`). If it does not, document the gap and either (a) drop hetty from the supported inspector list, (b) add a thin wrapper, or (c) escalate as a project decision.

If chaining is supported:
- Spawn hetty with upstream proxy config pointing at the connect filter port.
- Generate hetty config from InspectionRules (body-inspection subset only).
- Wire per-process CA via env (same as mitmproxy).
- Detect hetty availability; fail with clear error if missing.

**Parent epic:** AIDE-hze
**Depends on:** AIDE-hze.1, AIDE-dpe
**Design context:** docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-02-network-filter-egress-sandbox-design.md (Mode B section, Inspector feature matrix — flagged with verification requirement)

title: Mode B: mitmproxy Inspector (upstream mode through Filter, body-inspection addons)
description:
Implement the mitmproxy Inspector.

- Spawn mitmproxy with `--mode upstream:http://127.0.0.1:<filter-port>` so its egress chains through Mode A's connect filter.
- Generate Python addons from the InspectionRules (body-inspection only — no hostname rules; the filter handles those).
- Wire per-process CA via env (SSL_CERT_FILE, NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS, REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE).
- Detect mitmproxy availability; fail with clear error if missing.

**Parent epic:** AIDE-hze
**Depends on:** AIDE-hze.1 (Filter + Inspector interfaces) and AIDE-dpe (Mode A Filter must exist for chain to work)
**Design context:** docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-02-network-filter-egress-sandbox-design.md (Mode B section, Architecture chain diagram)

title: Mode B: define Filter + Inspector Go interfaces (split from monolithic Backend)
description:
Lock down the two internal Go interfaces:

- `Filter` — always present; single implementation (Mode A's connect proxy). Owns hostname rules, default policy, IP-literal handling, denial logging.
- `Inspector` — optional; multiple implementations (mitmproxy, hetty). Started in upstream-proxy mode pointing at `Filter`'s endpoint. Owns TLS termination + body inspection only. Hostname-blind.

Architecture chain in Mode B: agent → Inspector → Filter → internet.

**Parent epic:** AIDE-hze
**Design context:** docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-02-network-filter-egress-sandbox-design.md (Architecture section, Two-component model)

Replaces the original monolithic `Backend` interface — that conflated filtering and inspection roles, leading to triplicated rule logic across implementations. The split keeps hostname semantics in one place (our Go code) and lets inspectors focus on body inspection.

title: Kubernetes Verb Filter — destructive-op guard
description:
Opt-in capability that blocks destructive Kubernetes API verbs (Update, Delete, optionally Create) against a configurable cluster list. Provisions a wrapping kubeconfig for the agent that points at a local stripping proxy.

**Captured as future scope in:** docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-02-network-filter-egress-sandbox-design.md (Future scope, item 1)

## Why this epic exists separately from network filter
K8s filtering is **not** modeled as a feature of the network filter. The right shape is a capability-scoped tool wrapper that swaps the agent's kubeconfig for one pointing at a local proxy — protocol-aware filtering at the credential boundary, not the network boundary. This avoids MITMing kube-apiserver TLS and avoids re-implementing RBAC.

## Scope (high-level — needs its own brainstorm)
- Capability `k8s-readonly` (or equivalent name) that activates the wrapper
- Local kube-apiserver proxy that forwards GET/LIST and blocks Update/Delete/(optionally Create)
- Per-cluster allow rules (e.g. allow destructive on dev, block on prod)
- Wrapping kubeconfig generation
- Lifecycle supervision (reuse from Mode A/B)

## Depends on
Mode A epic (AIDE-dpe) — at minimum to land the supervision/env-wiring patterns this will reuse. Mode B (AIDE-hze) is not a hard dependency but informs the Backend abstraction shape.

## Next step
This is a placeholder epic. Run `/superpowers:brainstorming` against this epic to produce a real design before creating implementation tasks.

## Related future work (not part of this epic)
- SQL destructive-op filter
- MongoDB wire-protocol filter
- `go-plugin` extension point

title: Network Filter — Mode B: Deep Inspection (mitmproxy + hetty)
description:
Opt-in body-level inspection on top of Mode A. Decrypts HTTPS to allow inspectors over decoded bodies. Per-process CA only — no system trust store changes. Two Inspector implementations supported.

**Architecture (chained):** agent → Inspector (mitmproxy/hetty in upstream-proxy mode) → Filter (Mode A's connect proxy) → internet. Hostname filtering happens once at the Filter; the Inspector does TLS termination + body inspection only.

**Design:** docs/superpowers/specs/2026-05-02-network-filter-egress-sandbox-design.md

## Scope
- `Filter` + `Inspector` Go interface split (replaces monolithic Backend)
- mitmproxy Inspector: upstream-mode supervisor + Python addon generator (body inspection only)
- hetty Inspector: upstream-mode supervisor + Go config generator (verify chaining support first; subset of features)
- Per-process CA generation cached at ~/.local/share/aide/network-filter/ca/
- Per-process env wiring: SSL_CERT_FILE, NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS, REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE
- Default inspector: prompt-injection scanning of tool responses (allow/deny/annotate)
- Inspector feature matrix documented in user docs
- Coverage tests per supported agent
- `auto` Inspector selection logic

## Depends on
Mode A (AIDE-dpe) — Mode B chains through Mode A's Filter; the Filter must exist first.

## Out of scope
NeMo-Guardrails-style content rails on the agent↔Anthropic stream — explicitly rejected, see design Non-goals. K8s/SQL/Mongo filters — separate epics. `go-plugin` extension point — future scope.

title: Extract Function: secrets.LoadStore for decrypt-and-lookup pipeline
description: The pipeline 'config.ResolveSecretPath(name) -> secrets.DiscoverAgeKey() -> secrets.DecryptSecretsFile(path, identity) -> map lookup' is duplicated 8+ times across cmd/aide/env.go (envSetCmd RunE ~145-175 and selectSecretKey ~203-211), cmd/aide/secrets.go (lines 69-73, 89-91, 141-152), cmd/aide/status.go (240-243, 833-834, 897-901, 1129-1131), and internal/launcher/launcher.go (181-187). Each call site re-implements the same three-step decode, often with subtly different error handling. Extracting secrets.LoadStore(name string) (map[string]string, error) consolidates the seam, kills the package-level test vars (discoverAgeKey/decryptSecretsFile) in env.go, and makes future hooks (caching, alt key sources) one-line changes.
design:
TDD-first plan:
1. In internal/secrets, add a test file (manager_test.go or new loadstore_test.go) covering: (a) LoadStore returns decrypted map for a valid file; (b) LoadStore wraps DiscoverAgeKey error; (c) LoadStore wraps DecryptSecretsFile error; (d) name argument with and without .enc.yaml suffix both resolve correctly via config.ResolveSecretPath.
2. Implement secrets.LoadStore(name string) (map[string]string, error) in internal/secrets composing ResolveSecretPath + DiscoverAgeKey + DecryptSecretsFile. Confirm tests pass.
3. Replace call site in internal/launcher/launcher.go:178-192 with secrets.LoadStore. Run launcher tests.
4. Replace call sites in cmd/aide/status.go (240-253, 833-839, 896-944, 1124-1135) one block at a time. Run go build / go vet between edits.
5. Replace call sites in cmd/aide/secrets.go (secretsEditCmd lines 69-78 and 88-126, secretsKeysCmd lines 141-156). Run go build.
6. Replace call sites in cmd/aide/env.go: envSetCmd validation block (157-174) and selectSecretKey (203-211). Remove the package-level 'var discoverAgeKey' and 'var decryptSecretsFile' test seams; update cmd/aide/env_set_test.go to mock via a single secrets.LoadStore seam (or move the seam into internal/secrets).
7. Run full test suite (go test ./...) and confirm all green.
8. Commit.
acceptance_criteria: go test ./... passes. No call site outside internal/secrets calls secrets.DiscoverAgeKey + secrets.DecryptSecretsFile back-to-back. Package-level test seams in cmd/aide/env.go are removed.

Pattern Category: Composing Methods + Moving Features Between Objects
Pattern: Extract Function
Affected files: cmd/aide/env.go, cmd/aide/secrets.go, cmd/aide/status.go, internal/launcher/launcher.go, internal/secrets/manager.go (new function)
Confidence: high
Test coverage: partial (env_set_test.go covers env.go path via test seams; status.go and secrets.go have no _test.go siblings; launcher has launcher_test.go)

title: Show variant and provenance in startup banner
description: The aide startup banner displays capabilities like '✓ python' but does not reveal which variant actually applied. A user with uv.lock sees the same line as a user with .python-version, but the granted paths differ significantly (~/.local/share/uv vs ~/.pyenv). Users have to run 'aide cap consent list' to discover what variant is active — that's a surprise source and a trust-erosion risk. Provenance data is already computed by SelectVariants (Provenance{Variants, Reason}); just needs threading into the banner renderer.
design:
Banner render changes:

1. SelectVariants already returns Provenance{Variants []string, Reason string}. Collect these per-capability in the resolve step (launcher.go around line 254 in the Phase 4 wiring).

2. Thread a map[string]capability.Provenance through to the banner template context (internal/ui / display packages).

3. Update the banner format to include variants and provenance tag:

Current:
    ✓ python

Proposed:
    ✓ python[uv]                          (detected: uv.lock)

Multi-variant:
    ✓ python[uv + conda]                  (detected: uv.lock, environment.yml)

Non-default provenance:
    ✓ python[uv]                          (pinned via .aide.yaml)
    ✓ python[pyenv]                       (override via --variant)
    ✓ python[venv]                        (default: no markers detected)

4. Provenance tag mapping (short human-readable labels):
   - consent:granted -> 'detected: <summary>'
   - consent:stable  -> 'detected: <summary>'
   - yaml-pin        -> 'pinned via .aide.yaml'
   - cli-override    -> 'override via --variant'
   - default:no-evidence    -> 'default: no markers detected'
   - default:declined       -> 'default: consent declined'
   - default:skipped        -> 'default: skipped this launch'
   - default:non-interactive -> 'default: non-interactive session'

5. For capabilities without Variants, the banner stays as-is — no [variant] suffix.

TDD order:
1. Add a banner rendering test with fixture Provenance data asserting each provenance reason produces the right tag.
2. Thread Provenance through the resolve/apply path (keep Provenance stored somewhere the banner template can access).
3. Update banner template to emit the [variant] suffix and parenthetical tag.
4. Integration test: run 'aide' in a temp dir with uv.lock + --with python, assert banner contains '✓ python[uv]' and 'detected: uv.lock'.
acceptance_criteria: The banner output for a uv-detected python project contains both '[uv]' and the provenance tag 'detected: uv.lock'. For yaml-pin, cli-override, and default paths, the tag accurately reflects the source. Capabilities without Variants render unchanged.
notes: Real UX issue surfaced during v1.6.0 shake-out: user ran 'aide' and saw '✓ python' in the banner without any indication that the uv variant had been granted. Should ship soon after release but not a blocker for v1.6.0.
close_reason: Merged to main via 9 commits (59e847f → 65e3c19). CapabilityDisplay extended with Variants, ProvenanceTag, FreshGrant, EvidenceSummary, ConfirmedAt, DetectionHint. Three templates render tiered content: compact shows variant[uv] + 🆕 fresh-grant marker, clean adds (detected) provenance tag, boxed adds evidence: and confirmed: lines. Non-TTY output auto-downgrades to compact. Tests stable under -count=3.

title: Implement gpu capability for OpenGL/Metal/IOKit access
description: The 2026-04-14 toolchain-variant-mvp spec designed a gpu capability but the MVP rollout deferred it. ManimGL, PyTorch-Metal, JAX, and TensorFlow workloads currently fail under aide's seatbelt sandbox because Mach services and IOKit access are blocked. Users hit errors like 'cannot choose pixel format' with no way to grant the surface. Implement --with gpu as a strictly opt-in capability that grants the Mach/IOKit surface needed for GPU workloads.
design:
Design already captured in docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-14-generic-toolchain-detection-design.md under 'GPU capability' section:

  - New gpu entry in internal/capability/builtin.go with EnableGuard: [gpu]
  - New gpuGuard in pkg/seatbelt/guards/guard_gpu.go emitting SBPL for:
      (allow mach-lookup) for com.apple.windowserver.active,
        com.apple.iosurface, com.apple.cglcache,
        com.apple.CoreGraphics.*, com.apple.metal.*
      (allow iokit-open) for IOAccelerator, IOGPU, IOSurfaceRoot
      Read access to /System/Library/Frameworks/{OpenGL,Metal,CoreGraphics,IOKit}.framework
  - Guard type: opt-in (activated only when --with gpu present)
  - aide status warning banner when gpu active
  - Strictly no auto-detection, no consent flow — explicit flag only
  - Preflight may suggest --with gpu on moderngl/torch/jax/tensorflow imports but never activate it

TDD order:
1. Add a gpu-capability-not-auto-detected regression test (moderngl in pyproject.toml -> gpu NOT in auto-detected set).
2. Add the gpu capability to builtin.go with Markers: []Marker{} (zero — never auto-detects) and EnableGuard: [gpu].
3. Implement gpuGuard with the three rule groups.
4. Register the guard in registry.go as opt-in.
5. Add aide status warning when gpu is in the active set.
6. Integration test: --with gpu produces a sandbox profile that includes the expected mach-lookup/iokit-open/framework-read rules.
acceptance_criteria: --with gpu activates the gpu capability; the generated seatbelt profile contains mach-lookup rules for com.apple.windowserver.active, com.apple.iosurface, com.apple.metal.*; iokit-open for IOAccelerator/IOGPU/IOSurfaceRoot; read access to OpenGL/Metal/CoreGraphics/IOKit frameworks. aide status shows a warning when gpu is active. The capability is NEVER auto-detected from project markers (regression test guards this).
notes: Deferred from the toolchain-variant MVP. Not a blocker for v1.6.0 release. Should be tackled AFTER AIDE-72f (Marker polymorphism refactor) since that cleans up the detection engine first. Original motivation: SANDBOX-ISSUES.md documents ManimGL blocked on 'cannot choose pixel format' error.

title: Replace Conditional with Polymorphism: Marker as interface
description: Marker is a sum type simulated with three mutually-exclusive fields (File/Contains/GlobPath). Four parallel three-way switches re-discriminate by inspecting which field is non-empty: Marker.Validate, Marker.Match, Marker.MatchSummary, markerKind in detect_variants.go. Each new marker kind (e.g. DirExists when DetectProject unification lands) requires touching all four sites. Make Marker an interface with concrete FileMarker/ContainsMarker/GlobMarker types each providing Kind/Validate/Match/Summary. Design implication: YAML unmarshaling needs a custom UnmarshalYAML on a wrapper struct to pick the concrete type.
design:
TDD order:
1. Keep existing variant_test.go and detect_variants_test.go passing throughout.
2. Write a new test declaring markers via the interface and exercising all kinds.
3. Introduce the interface alongside the existing struct form; add a wrapper type with UnmarshalYAML that dispatches.
4. Port call sites (Match/Validate/MatchSummary/markerKind) to the interface.
5. Delete struct form once all tests pass and no callers remain.
acceptance_criteria: No regression in variant/detection tests; adding a new marker kind requires only a new type plus a single unmarshal case, not edits to 4 switches; YAML round-trip preserved.
notes: Pattern: Replace Conditional with Polymorphism (Strategy). Coordinate with AIDE-c7o (DetectProject unification) — if DetectProject is re-expressed as Markers, DirExists becomes the 4th switch arm that motivates this.

title: Encapsulate grant body format in a single serializer
description: In internal/consent/consent.go, the (project, capability, variants) tuple is serialized three different ways: (a) length-prefixed SHA input in ConsentHash, (b) line-oriented key:value text body in Grant, (c) strings.HasPrefix on body text in Revoke and List, (d) SplitN+switch parser in parseGrantBody. Revoke's prefix-match is fragile — adding a field in a non-prefix position or reordering fields silently breaks it. Encapsulate the body encode/decode into a dedicated grantBody type.
design:
TDD order:
1. consent_test.go already covers Grant/Revoke/List roundtrips.
2. Add a test asserting Revoke still matches grants when an optional field is added to the body in a non-prefix position — captures the fragility.
3. Introduce grantBody type with Encode/Decode/MatchesProject/MatchesProjectCap.
4. Route Grant/Revoke/List/parseGrantBody through it.
5. Run tests; the new robustness test should now pass.
acceptance_criteria: Revoke and List work when body field order is rearranged; existing consent tests pass unchanged. ConsentHash input encoding (length-prefixed) is NOT touched — that encoding is intentionally aggregate-specific and collision-safe.
notes: Pattern: DRY / Encapsulate Collection(of-format). Does NOT touch trust.FileHash/PathHash — those serve a different shape without collision risk and are not duplicates.

title: Consolidate bounded file-read helpers in internal/capability
description: Three near-identical 64KB bounded substring-in-file readers live in internal/capability: variant.go:containsInBoundedFile, detect.go:containsInFile(dir,filename,substr), detect.go:containsInFileByPath(path,substr). Each defines its own size constant (markerMaxReadSize vs maxReadSize). Additionally, Marker.Match's File branch reimplements fileExists inline via os.Stat+!fi.IsDir(), duplicating detect.go:fileExists. Consolidate into one shared set of primitives with one size constant.
design:
TDD order:
1. Keep existing tests in detect_test.go and variant_test.go green throughout.
2. Introduce fileContains(path, pattern string) bool and single const maxReadBytes used by both files.
3. Rewrite containsInFile/containsInFileByPath/containsInBoundedFile as one-liners over fileContains, or replace callers directly.
4. Replace the inline os.Stat+!IsDir in Marker.Match with a call to fileExists.
5. Tests rerun unchanged.
acceptance_criteria: Only one bounded-read implementation and one size constant survive in internal/capability; Marker.Match uses fileExists; all existing tests pass without modification.
notes: Pattern: Replace Inline Code with Function Call. Test coverage: partial (internal helpers not directly tested).
close_reason: AIDE-c7o: merged to main via 7 commits (ee8b862 → e579dbc) — DetectProject rewritten as registry loop, 8 ad-hoc helpers deleted, 2 new marker kinds (DirExists/GlobContains), fs.FS throughout, golden test unchanged. AIDE-2kg: resolved as side-effect — variant.go's containsInBoundedFileFS is now the single bounded-read primitive with one 64KB cap.

title: Extract loadCapRegistry helper for cap subcommand preamble
description: cmd/aide/commands.go repeats os.Getwd -> config.Load(config.Dir(), cwd) -> capability.FromConfigDefs(cfg.Capabilities) -> capability.MergedRegistry(userCaps) in ~25 cap subcommand RunE bodies (capList, capShow, capVariants, capConsentList, capConsentRevoke, and many others). Shotgun surgery risk: when registry construction changes (e.g. node/ruby migrate to variant-based detection), every call site must be updated. Extract a helper.

4. Refactor call sites in batches of 5, running tests after each batch.
5. Verify integration tests still green.

notes: Pattern: Extract Function / Shotgun Surgery. Test coverage: partial.
close_reason: Merged to main via 21 commits (5474a44 → b6072f4). commands.go 4927 → 200 lines split across 9 per-subject files (cap/context/env/sandbox/secrets/agents/trust/config/status). cmdEnv(cmd) helper in cmdenv.go with lazy+memoized Registry(). 35 config.Load sites collapsed; 3 remaining documented with inline comments. Tests green, no commit leaks.

title: Document keychain write access design decision
description: Record the threat analysis and rationale for granting file-write* on ~/Library/Keychains in the keychain guard. The change was needed because the read-only keychain guard prevented OAuth token refresh, causing session logout after TTL expiry. Threat analysis shows API-level isolation (securityd ACLs) prevents cross-app credential access regardless of file-level permissions.
design:
DD: Keychain guard grants file-write* on ~/Library/Keychains subpath.

Rationale: Read-only keychain access prevented OAuth token refresh, causing session logout after TTL expiry. file-write* is safe because: (1) credential isolation is enforced at API level by securityd ACLs tied to code signatures, not at filesystem level; (2) cross-app credential access/modification is blocked by securityd regardless of file permissions; (3) remaining risk is DB file corruption (requires prior compromise, recoverable from backup).

Alternatives tried: Narrow literal scope on login.keychain-db failed because securityd creates .sb-* temp files alongside the DB.
notes: Correction: version: 512 in keychain output is the file format version (0x200), not content revision history. No built-in keychain versioning exists. Recovery from corruption depends on Time Machine or iCloud Keychain sync. securityd holds a file lock on the DB which prevents casual overwrites while the daemon is running.
close_reason: Committed as 47bd734, tagged v1.4.4. Keychain write access granted via subpath, threat analysis documented in issue design field.

title: PARKED: Revisit what --resolve flag actually does
description:
## Idea
Claimed during debugging that 'aide --resolve --with git-remote' prints the banner without launching the agent. Wrong — the test run still attempted to exec the agent and ended with 'sandbox-exec: sandbox_apply: Operation not permitted'. The --resolve flag's behavior is ambiguous.

## What to investigate
- What does --resolve actually do today? (read cmd/aide root command + launcher)
- Help text just says 'Show detailed startup info' — does it short-circuit the launch, print extra info before launch, or something else?
- Should there be a true dry-run mode that prints the banner + exits 0? (useful for testing banner/hint behavior like the SSH discoverability hint we just shipped)
- If we add dry-run, what's the right flag name? --dry-run, --no-launch, repurpose --resolve, or new flag?
- Update help text to match actual behavior

## Context
Parked while debugging the missing-hint report (user installed aide but did not see the SSH hint in the banner). Built the binary and ran it to verify the hint code path; mistakenly characterized --resolve as a non-launching mode.

title: PARKED: Improve visibility of untrusted .aide.yaml warning
description:
## Idea
When .aide.yaml is not trusted, the warning is hardly visible and easy to miss.

Current output example:
```
aide
! /Users/subramk/source/github.com/tails-mpt/redfish-simulator/.aide.yaml is not trusted
```

The single `!` prefix gets lost in normal output. Users may not notice that the config they expect to be loaded is being ignored.

## Suggested Improvements
- Use color (yellow/red) for the warning
- Add a clear icon/banner (e.g. ⚠️  or boxed warning)
- Include a hint on how to trust it (e.g. 'aide trust <path>')
- Make it stand out from regular log lines

## Context
No active task. Triggered by user noticing the untrusted-config warning is too subtle to be useful.

title: Introduce typed errors for config.Load failures
description: config.Load returns a flat error today, so callers using best-effort loading (cfg, _ := config.Load(...)) silently swallow every failure — missing file, malformed YAML, filesystem permission errors, and trust-denied all produce the same outcome. Introduce typed errors (e.g. ConfigNotFoundError, ConfigParseError, ConfigFilesystemError, ConfigTrustDeniedError) so callers can distinguish benign vs real failures and act accordingly.
design:
Two implementation options to evaluate:
1. Wrap errors inside internal/cmdenv (or wherever Load() lives post-AIDE-4ud). The classification is a thin post-hoc check over config.Load's returned error (string match or sentinel check). Small blast radius.
2. Push typed errors down into internal/config itself. Larger blast radius but broader benefit (any consumer of config.Load gets the distinction).

TDD order:
1. Identify first consumer — likely the best-effort sites (e.g. capAudit, secretsList) that silently ignore every error today. Each one has a preferred policy: missing file is fine, other failures should surface.
2. Define error taxonomy with tests asserting each category is returned for the right failure mode.
3. Update one consumer to use errors.As / errors.Is and demonstrate the improved UX.
4. Roll out to remaining best-effort consumers.
acceptance_criteria: At least one concrete consumer distinguishes ConfigNotFoundError from other errors; all existing config.Load callers keep working (backward-compatible unwrap via errors.Is/As).
notes: Deferred from AIDE-4ud brainstorm on 2026-04-15: the fluent Load() loader is being introduced with a flat error signature because no current caller distinguishes error kinds. Typed errors would be a net positive but need a first consumer to justify them — tackling this separately with its own design pass.

title: PARKED: Domain/URL-level network filtering via local proxy
description:
## Idea
Domain/URL-level network filtering for aide sandbox via local proxy. Requires running a local HTTP/HTTPS proxy, setting HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY in agent env, and implementing allow/deny domain lists.

Design the schema now (Option B style: allow_domains, deny_domains) but implementation is a separate epic. Reference: Anthropic's sandbox-runtime uses this pattern.

Example config:
```yaml
sandbox:
  network:
    mode: outbound
```
